lists.title: Spanish A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar in Plan Curricular (Instituto Cervantes) order: pronouns and nouns first, then articles, the two copulas, present-tense verb families, negation, existence, and questions.

lists.title: Spanish A2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Everyday past and preferences: reflexives, gustar, the two pasts, plans, progressive, object pronouns, comparison.

lists.title: Spanish B1 Grammar Path
lists.description: The independent-speaker layer: perfect, future, conditional, first subjunctive, commands, por/para, se, clitic clusters.

lists.title: Spanish B2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Complex sentences: past subjunctive, conditionals, pluperfect, reported speech, precise relatives, purpose/concession.

lists.title: Spanish C1 Grammar Path
lists.description: Formal register: perfect and pluperfect subjunctive, ser-passive, clefts.

lists.title: Spanish C2 Grammar Path
lists.description: Native-like nuance: forceful concession, literary -se forms, layered denial.

points.title: Subject pronouns (yo, tú, él…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about
points.explanation: The subject pronouns are: yo (I), tú (you, informal), él/ella (he/she), usted (you, formal), nosotros/nosotras (we), vosotros/vosotras (you all, Spain), ellos/ellas (they), ustedes (you all). Because the verb ending already shows the person, Spanish usually DROPS the pronoun — you say it mainly for emphasis or contrast: 'Yo soy de México (pero él no)'.
points.culture_note: tú vs usted matters socially: tú with friends, family, and peers; usted with strangers, elders, and formal situations. Regions differ — much of Latin America uses ustedes for ALL plural 'you'.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish pronouns

drills.translation: I am from Mexico.
You are my friend.
She is my sister.
We are students.
They are from Spain.
He is my father.
Are you Dr. Ruiz?
You all are from Valencia.
You (all) are welcome.
I speak a little Spanish.
Do you live here?
He works in a bank.
She sings very well.
Do you (formal) speak English?
We live in Madrid.
Are you all hungry?
They play football.
Do you (all, formal) need help?

related.title: The verb ser (to be) — present
related.contrast: The verb ending already names the person, so yo/tú usually drop.

related.title: Present tense of -ar verbs
related.contrast: Hablo means 'I speak' — no pronoun needed.

points.title: Gender and number of nouns
points.function: Name people and things, one or many
points.explanation: Every Spanish noun is masculine or feminine. Most nouns in -o are masculine (libro) and most in -a are feminine (casa). Plurals add -s after a vowel (casa → casas) and -es after a consonant (ciudad → ciudades). Gender and number ripple through the whole sentence — articles and adjectives must match the noun.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish nouns

drills.translation: I have two books.
The houses are pretty.
I have three sisters.
The cars are new.
The tables are small.
I have two cats at home.

related.title: Definite articles (el / la)
related.contrast: El/la must match the noun's gender: el día, la mano.

related.title: Adjective agreement
related.contrast: Adjectives copy the noun's gender AND number: casas blancas.

points.title: Definite articles (el / la)
points.explanation: Spanish nouns have grammatical gender. Use 'el' before masculine singular nouns and 'la' before feminine singular nouns; the plurals are 'los' and 'las'. Most nouns ending in -o are masculine (el libro) and most ending in -a are feminine (la casa), but there are exceptions you learn word by word.
points.culture_note: Gender is grammatical, not literal — 'el mapa' (the map) is masculine despite ending in -a, a Greek-derived exception.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish determiners — articles

drills.translation: The house is big.
The book is new.
The houses are white.
The teacher is nice.
The cars are fast.
The school is big.
The children play in the park.
The flowers are beautiful.

related.title: Indefinite articles (un / una)
related.contrast: El points at a known thing; un introduces a new one.

related.title: Gender and number of nouns
related.contrast: The article is your best clue to a noun's gender.

points.title: Indefinite articles (un / una)
points.function: Mention something for the first time
points.explanation: 'Un' goes before masculine singular nouns and 'una' before feminine singular nouns: un perro, una casa. The plurals 'unos/unas' mean 'some'. Use the indefinite article to introduce something new; after that, switch to el/la because it is now known.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wiktionary: un (Spanish)

drills.translation: I have a dog.
It is a big house.
I am buying a new table.
There is a problem with the car.
I have an idea.
It is a beautiful day.

related.title: Definite articles (el / la)
related.contrast: Un for first mention, el once it's known.

related.title: Hay — there is / there are
related.contrast: Hay pairs with un/una, never with el/la: hay una mesa.

points.title: The verb ser (to be) — present
points.function: Say who or what someone is
points.explanation: 'Ser' expresses permanent or defining qualities: identity, origin, profession. Present forms: soy, eres, es, somos, sois, son. Use ser for who/what something is (Soy estudiante), and the separate verb estar for temporary states and location.
points.culture_note: Spanish has two verbs for 'to be' — ser (essence) and estar (state). 'Es aburrido' = he is boring; 'está aburrido' = he is bored.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish verbs — ser and estar
Wiktionary: ser (Spanish)
Ser and estar
references.book: Butt & Benjamin, A New Reference Grammar of Modern Spanish

drills.translation: I am a student.
We are friends.
She is a teacher.
They are from Argentina.
You are very tall.
You (all) are my friends.
I am from Colombia.
Are you a doctor?
My mother is a teacher.
We are brothers.
You all are very nice.
My parents are from Seville.

related.title: The verb estar — location and states
related.contrast: Ser = what something IS; estar = how or where it is right now.

related.title: Adjective agreement
related.contrast: Description with ser: la casa es blanca — the adjective still agrees.

points.title: The verb estar — location and states
points.function: Say where things are and how you feel
points.explanation: 'Estar' covers location and temporary states: estoy, estás, está, estamos, estáis, están. Location ALWAYS takes estar, even for permanent places: 'Madrid está en España'. States that can change — tired, cold, happy — also take estar: 'Estoy cansado'.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wiktionary: estar (Spanish)
Ser and estar
references.book: Butt & Benjamin, A New Reference Grammar of Modern Spanish

drills.translation: I am at home.
The food is cold.
We are in the park.
Where are my keys?
You are tired today.
The coffee is hot.
You all are in class.
I am very tired today.
Where are you now?
We are ready.
Are you all well?
The plates are on the table.

related.title: The verb ser (to be) — present
related.contrast: Estar for states and places; ser for identity and origin.

related.title: Hay — there is / there are
related.contrast: Hay says something exists; está says where a KNOWN thing is.

points.title: Present tense of -ar verbs
points.explanation: Regular -ar verbs (hablar, estudiar, trabajar) drop -ar and add endings: -o, -as, -a, -amos, -áis, -an. So hablar → hablo, hablas, habla, hablamos, habláis, hablan.
points.culture_note: -ar verbs are the largest and most regular Spanish verb group, so mastering this pattern unlocks hundreds of verbs at once.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish conjugation — present indicative
Wiktionary: hablar (conjugation)

drills.translation: We speak Spanish.
She works in an office.
I listen to music at night.
They travel a lot in summer.
Do you study English?
My mother prepares dinner.
Do you all speak Spanish?
I work in a hospital.
What music do you listen to?
We prepare the food together.
Where do you all work?
My friends study French.

related.title: Present tense of -er and -ir verbs
related.contrast: Same person endings, different theme vowel: hablas vs comes.

related.title: Negation with no
related.contrast: No sits right before the conjugated verb: no hablo.

points.title: Present tense of -er and -ir verbs
points.function: Talk about eating, drinking, living, and more
points.explanation: -er verbs (comer, beber) take -o, -es, -e, -emos, -éis, -en; -ir verbs (vivir, escribir) are the same except nosotros/vosotros: -imos, -ís. So como, comes, come, comemos… and vivo, vives, vive, vivimos…

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wiktionary: comer (conjugation)
Wiktionary: vivir (conjugation)

drills.translation: I drink water.
We live in Madrid.
She reads many books.
They live in Barcelona.
I write letters to my grandmother.
We eat bread in the morning.
Do you eat meat?
You all live near the beach.
What do you usually drink?
My brother lives in Valencia.
What are you all reading?
The children eat a lot of bread.

related.title: Present tense of -ar verbs
related.contrast: -er and -ir share endings except nosotros/vosotros.

related.title: Subject pronouns (yo, tú, él…)
related.contrast: Comes = 'you eat' — the ending carries the person.

points.title: Negation with no
points.explanation: Put 'no' directly before the conjugated verb: 'No tengo tiempo' (I don't have time), 'Ella no habla inglés'. Answering a question negatively often doubles it: '¿Hablas francés? — No, no hablo francés.'

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish grammar — negation

drills.translation: I don't have time.
She doesn't speak English.
I don't drink coffee.
They don't live here.
We are not from Madrid.
She is not at home.

related.title: Present tense of -ar verbs
related.contrast: Negation never splits verb and ending: no trabajo.

related.title: Hay — there is / there are
related.contrast: No hay = there isn't any.

points.title: Hay — there is / there are
points.function: Say what exists or is available
points.explanation: 'Hay' (from haber) is invariable: it means both 'there is' and 'there are'. 'Hay un libro en la mesa', 'Hay tres cafés', 'No hay leche'. Use hay for existence; use estar to locate something specific you already know about.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wiktionary: hay (Spanish)

drills.translation: There is a book on the table.
There is no milk.
There are many people in the street.
Is there a bank nearby?
There is no hot water.
There are two windows in my room.

related.title: The verb estar — location and states
related.contrast: Hay introduces; está locates what's already introduced.

related.title: Indefinite articles (un / una)
related.contrast: Hay un problema — indefinite, because it's new information.

points.title: Question words (qué, dónde, cómo…)
points.function: Ask about things, places, people, and time
points.explanation: The interrogatives carry a written accent: qué (what), dónde (where), cómo (how), quién (who), cuándo (when), cuánto (how much). Questions are wrapped in ¿…?: '¿Dónde estás?', '¿Cómo te llamas?'. Word order stays simple — question word + verb.
points.culture_note: The upside-down ¿ opens every question in writing — it tells the reader the intonation before the sentence starts.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish grammar — interrogatives

drills.translation: Where are you?
What is your name? (lit. how do you call yourself)
What is this?
Where do you live?
When is your birthday?
How old are you?

related.title: The verb ser (to be) — present
related.contrast: ¿Qué es esto? — identity questions use ser.

related.title: The verb estar — location and states
related.contrast: ¿Dónde está…? — location questions use estar.

points.title: Adjective agreement
points.explanation: Adjectives match their noun in gender and number: casa blanca, libros nuevos. Adjectives in -o have four forms (blanco/blanca/blancos/blancas); adjectives in -e or a consonant only change for number (grande/grandes, azul/azules). Most adjectives FOLLOW the noun.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish adjectives

drills.translation: The house is white.
The books are new.
The car is red.
The tables are small.
My sister is very nice.
The children are tired.
The dog is small.
The windows are open.

related.title: Gender and number of nouns
related.contrast: The noun sets gender and number; the adjective follows.

related.title: The verb ser (to be) — present
related.contrast: After ser the adjective still agrees with the subject.

points.title: Reflexive verbs (levantarse)
points.explanation: Reflexive verbs carry a pronoun that points back at the subject: me levanto (I get up), te duchas, se viste, nos acostamos. The pronoun goes before the conjugated verb, and the infinitive keeps -se: levantarse.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish pronouns — reflexive

drills.translation: I get up at seven.
She goes to bed early.
We get up late on Sundays.
What time do you shower?

drills.sentence: Él se {{answer}} los dientes después de comer.
drills.translation: He brushes his teeth after eating.
My name is Carmen.

points.title: Gustar and similar verbs
points.function: Say what you like and dislike
points.explanation: Gustar works backwards: the thing liked is the SUBJECT, so the verb agrees with it — Me gusta el café (coffee pleases me), Me gustan los libros (books please me). The person appears as me/te/le/nos/os/les. Encantar, interesar, and doler follow the same pattern.
points.culture_note: Learners who say 'yo gusto café' get corrected fast — thinking 'X pleases me' instead of 'I like X' is the unlock.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wiktionary: gustar (Spanish)

drills.translation: I like coffee.
I like books.
We like travelling by train.
Do you like horror films?
My head hurts.
He/she likes chocolate a lot.

points.title: Preterite — regular verbs
points.explanation: The preterite reports completed past events: hablar → hablé, hablaste, habló, hablamos, hablasteis, hablaron; -er/-ir verbs share endings: comí, comiste, comió… Ayer hablé con mi madre (Yesterday I spoke with my mother).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish conjugation — preterite

drills.translation: Yesterday I spoke with my mother.
She opened the door.
Last night I wrote a letter.
They bought the house in 2010.
Did you speak with the teacher yesterday?

drills.sentence: Nosotros {{answer}} en un restaurante el sábado.
drills.translation: We ate at a restaurant on Saturday.

points.title: Preterite — key irregulars (fui, hice, tuve)
points.function: Tell what happened with the most common verbs
points.explanation: The most frequent verbs are irregular in the preterite: ser/ir share fui, fuiste, fue…; hacer → hice, hizo; tener → tuve; estar → estuve; poder → pude. They carry no written accent: fue, hizo.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wiktionary: ir (Spanish conjugation)

drills.translation: Yesterday I went to the cinema.
He had a lot of work.
Yesterday it was very cold.
She was a teacher for many years.
We didn't have time.
What did you do at the weekend?

points.title: Imperfect (hablaba)
points.function: Describe how things used to be
points.explanation: The imperfect paints background and habits: -ar verbs take -aba (hablaba, jugaba), -er/-ir take -ía (comía, vivía). Only three verbs are irregular: era, iba, veía. Cuando era niño, jugaba mucho (When I was a child, I played a lot).
points.culture_note: Preterite vs imperfect is THE Spanish past-tense contrast: what happened (preterite) against what was going on (imperfect).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish verbs — imperfect

drills.translation: When I was a child, I used to play a lot.
We used to live in Madrid.
My grandmother used to tell wonderful stories.

drills.sentence: De niños, {{answer}} al fútbol cada tarde.
drills.translation: As children, we played football every afternoon.
When you arrived, I was watching television.
The city used to be quieter.

points.title: Ir a + infinitive (near future)
points.explanation: Plans and near future use ir a + infinitive: Voy a viajar mañana (I'm going to travel tomorrow), Vamos a comer (We're going to eat). Only ir conjugates; the second verb stays an infinitive.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish verbs — periphrases

drills.translation: I am going to travel tomorrow.
They are going to eat.
She is going to organise a party.
We are going to study tonight.
Are you going to come tomorrow?

drills.sentence: Voy a {{answer}} pescado para la cena.
drills.translation: I am going to buy fish for dinner.

points.title: Estar + gerund (present progressive)
points.function: Say what is happening right now
points.explanation: Right-now actions use estar + gerund: -ando for -ar verbs (hablando), -iendo for -er/-ir (comiendo, viviendo; leyendo and durmiendo are irregular). Estoy leyendo un libro (I am reading a book).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish verbs — gerund

drills.translation: I am reading a book.
She is sleeping.

drills.sentence: Los niños están {{answer}} en el jardín.
drills.translation: The children are playing in the garden.
What are you doing?
We are preparing dinner now.

drills.sentence: Ella está {{answer}} con su madre por teléfono.
drills.translation: She is talking with her mother on the phone.

points.title: Direct object pronouns (lo, la, los, las)
points.function: Avoid repeating what you already mentioned
points.explanation: Replace known direct objects with lo/la/los/las agreeing with the noun: ¿El libro? Lo tengo (I have it). The pronoun goes before the conjugated verb, or attaches to an infinitive/gerund: quiero verla.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish pronouns — object

drills.translation: The book? I have it.
The keys? I don't see them.
The film? We saw it last night.
The books? I bought them yesterday.
The bread? I can't find it.
The photos? I have them on my phone.

points.title: Indirect object pronouns (le, les)
points.function: Say to whom or for whom
points.explanation: The receiver is marked with me/te/le/nos/os/les, usually doubled with a + person: Le di el libro a Juan (I gave the book to Juan), Les escribí a mis padres. Le/les don't mark gender.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish pronouns — object

drills.translation: I gave the book to Juan.
I wrote to my parents.
I bought flowers for my mother.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} conté la historia a los niños.
drills.translation: I told the story to the children.
Did you give the keys to Ana?

drills.sentence: El profesor {{answer}} explica la lección a los alumnos.
drills.translation: The teacher explains the lesson to the students.

points.title: Comparatives (más… que, tan… como)
points.explanation: More/less: más/menos + adjective + que — Ella es más alta que yo. Equality: tan + adjective + como — Es tan alto como su padre. Irregulars: mejor (better), peor (worse), mayor, menor.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática A1–A2
Wikipedia: Spanish grammar — comparison

drills.translation: She is taller than me.
He is as tall as his father.

drills.sentence: El tren es {{answer}} rápido que el autobús.
drills.translation: The train is faster than the bus.

drills.sentence: Mi casa es {{answer}} grande que la tuya.
drills.translation: My house is smaller (less big) than yours.

drills.sentence: Ella canta tan bien {{answer}} su hermana.
drills.translation: She sings as well as her sister.

drills.sentence: Este libro es {{answer}} que el otro.
drills.translation: This book is better than the other one.

points.title: Present perfect (he hablado)
points.explanation: Haber (he, has, ha, hemos, habéis, han) + participle (-ado/-ido): Hoy he trabajado mucho (Today I've worked a lot). Common irregular participles: hecho, dicho, visto, escrito, puesto, vuelto.
points.culture_note: Spain uses the present perfect for today's events (hoy he comido); much of Latin America prefers the preterite (hoy comí). Both are correct.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish verbs — compound tenses

drills.translation: Today I have worked a lot.
Have you seen that film?
I have never been to Mexico.
Have you (all) eaten already?

drills.sentence: Ella ha {{answer}} una carta muy larga.
drills.translation: She has written a very long letter.
This year we have travelled a lot.

points.title: Future simple (hablaré)
points.explanation: Attach -é, -ás, -á, -emos, -éis, -án to the whole infinitive: hablaré, comerás, vivirá. A few stems change: tendré, haré, podré, saldré. It also expresses guesses: Serán las diez (It must be around ten).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish conjugation — future

drills.translation: Tomorrow I will speak with you.
They will arrive late.

drills.sentence: El año que viene {{answer}} a España.
drills.translation: Next year I will go to Spain.
What will the weather be like tomorrow?
We will know the truth.

drills.sentence: Ella {{answer}} una casa junto al mar.
drills.translation: She will have a house by the sea.

points.title: Conditional (hablaría)
points.function: Say what you would do; soften requests
points.explanation: Infinitive + -ía endings: hablaría, comerías, viviríamos; the future's irregular stems repeat: tendría, haría. Uses: hypotheticals (Viajaría por el mundo) and politeness (¿Podrías ayudarme?).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish conjugation — conditional

drills.translation: I would like to travel the world.
What would you do in my place?

drills.sentence: Yo en tu lugar no {{answer}} eso.
drills.translation: In your place I wouldn't say that.
Could you help me with this, please?

drills.sentence: Nosotros {{answer}} más tiempo con la familia.
drills.translation: We would spend more time with family.

drills.sentence: Él dijo que {{answer}} a las ocho.
drills.translation: He said he would arrive at eight.

points.title: Present subjunctive — wishes and requests
points.explanation: After querer que, es importante que, ojalá…, the second verb goes to the subjunctive: -ar verbs flip to -e endings (hables), -er/-ir to -a (comas, vengas). Quiero que vengas temprano (I want you to come early).
points.culture_note: The subjunctive is where Spanish 'feels different': it marks wanting, doubting, and valuing rather than stating.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish subjunctive

drills.translation: I want you to come early.
It's important that you drink water.
I hope you have a good trip.
They want us to speak more slowly.

drills.sentence: Es necesario que ella {{answer}} al médico.
drills.translation: It is necessary that she go to the doctor.
I don't want them to know it.

points.title: Imperative — affirmative and negative (tú)
points.explanation: Affirmative tú = third-person present (¡Abre la puerta!), with irregulars ven, haz, di, pon, sal. NEGATIVE commands switch to the subjunctive: ¡No hagas eso!, ¡No vengas tarde! Pronouns attach after affirmatives (dímelo) but precede negatives (no me lo digas).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish verbs — imperative

drills.translation: Open the door!
Don't do that!
Speak more slowly, please!
Don't open the window!
Come here right now!
Don't tell me lies!

points.title: Por vs para
points.explanation: Para points forward: purpose, destination, deadline — Este regalo es para ti; Salgo para Madrid. Por looks at cause, exchange, movement through, and means — Gracias por tu ayuda; Pasamos por el parque; por teléfono.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish prepositions

drills.translation: This gift is for you.
Thanks for your help.
I study Spanish in order to work in Chile.
We walked through the park.
The present is for my mother.
I bought it for ten euros.

points.title: Impersonal and passive se
points.explanation: Se + third person removes the doer: Se habla español (Spanish is spoken / one speaks Spanish), Aquí se venden frutas (fruit is sold here — verb agrees with the noun). It's the everyday passive; ser + participle is more formal.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish grammar — se

drills.translation: Spanish is spoken here.
Fruit is sold here.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} come muy bien en este restaurante.
drills.translation: One eats very well at this restaurant.
Houses are rented in this neighbourhood.
How do you say this in Spanish?
Spanish is spoken in twenty countries.

points.title: Double object pronouns (se lo)
points.explanation: Indirect before direct: me lo, te la… When both are third person, le/les becomes SE: Le di el libro → Se lo di (I gave it to him). Order is fixed: se lo/la/los/las.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish pronouns — clitic combinations

drills.sentence: ¿El libro? {{answer}} lo di ayer a Juan.
drills.translation: The book? I gave it to Juan yesterday.
drills.hint: the indirect pronoun changes shape before lo/la

drills.sentence: El regalo, se {{answer}} di a María.
drills.translation: The gift — I gave it to María.
The keys? I gave them to the doorman.

drills.sentence: El coche, {{answer}} lo vendí a mi vecino.
drills.translation: The car, I sold it to my neighbour.
drills.hint: the indirect pronoun changes shape before lo/la

drills.sentence: ¿Me prestas el libro? — Sí, te {{answer}} presto.
drills.translation: Will you lend me the book? — Yes, I'll lend it to you.

drills.sentence: ¿El regalo? {{answer}} lo daremos mañana a la abuela.
drills.translation: The present? We'll give it to grandma tomorrow.
drills.hint: the indirect pronoun changes shape before lo/la

points.title: Past (imperfect) subjunctive (hablara)
points.function: Talk about unreal or reported wants
points.explanation: Built from the ellos-preterite minus -ron plus -ra endings: hablara, tuviera, viniera (an -se variant exists). Triggered by past-tense wanting (Quería que vinieras) and unreal conditions (Si tuviera dinero…).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish subjunctive — imperfect

drills.translation: If I had money, I would travel.
I wanted you to come with me.
If I had more time, I would learn French.

drills.sentence: Me pidió que le {{answer}} con la mudanza.
drills.translation: He asked me to help him with the move.
I wish my brother were here.
They were looking for someone who spoke Russian.

points.title: Si-clauses (real and unreal)
points.explanation: Real: si + present → future (Si estudias, aprobarás). Unreal present: si + past subjunctive → conditional (Si tuviera, viajaría). Unreal past: si + pluperfect subjunctive → conditional perfect (Si hubiera sabido, no habría venido).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish grammar — conditional sentences

drills.translation: If you study, you will pass.
Had I known, I would not have come.
If it rains tomorrow, we won't go out.
If I were rich, I would buy an island.
If it's cold, close the window.
If you had called me, I would have come.

points.title: Pluperfect (había hablado)
points.explanation: Había, habías… + participle marks a past before another past: Cuando llegué, ya había salido (When I arrived, she had already left).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish verbs — compound tenses

drills.translation: When I arrived, she had already left.
We had never seen the sea.
We had already eaten when they arrived.

drills.sentence: Ella nunca {{answer}} viajado en avión antes.
drills.translation: She had never travelled by plane before.

drills.sentence: Cuando volví, ya habían {{answer}} la puerta.
drills.translation: When I returned, they had already closed the door.

drills.sentence: Me di cuenta de que {{answer}} perdido las llaves.
drills.translation: I realised that I had lost the keys.

points.title: Reported speech (dijo que…)
points.explanation: Shifting into the past moves tenses back: 'Estoy cansado' → Dijo que estaba cansado; 'Vendré' → Dijo que vendría; commands become subjunctive: Me dijo que viniera.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Indirect speech — Spanish

drills.translation: He said he was tired.
She told me she would come tomorrow.
He said he couldn't come today.

drills.sentence: Me contó que {{answer}} vivido en Perú.
drills.translation: She told me she had lived in Peru.
They said they would arrive on Monday.
He asked where the hospital was.

points.title: Relative pronouns (quien, cuyo)
points.explanation: Beyond que: quien(es) after prepositions for people (la mujer con quien hablé), cuyo/a/os/as = whose, agreeing with the THING owned (el autor cuyo libro leí), and el que / lo que for selection and ideas.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish grammar — relative clauses

drills.sentence: La mujer con {{answer}} hablé es doctora.
drills.translation: The woman with whom I spoke is a doctor.

drills.sentence: El autor, {{answer}} libro leí, es chileno.
drills.translation: The author, whose book I read, is Chilean.

drills.sentence: El médico a {{answer}} llamamos llegó rápido.
drills.translation: The doctor whom we called arrived quickly.

drills.sentence: La casa, {{answer}} puertas son rojas, es antigua.
drills.translation: The house, whose doors are red, is old.

drills.sentence: Los estudiantes, {{answer}} trabajos leí, son brillantes.
drills.translation: The students, whose papers I read, are brilliant.
It was she who solved the problem.

points.title: Subjunctive after para que, aunque…
points.explanation: Purpose always takes subjunctive: Te lo digo para que lo sepas (…so that you know). Aunque + subjunctive concedes something unconfirmed: Aunque llueva, iremos (Even if it rains, we'll go) — with indicative it reports fact (Aunque llueve… = even though it IS raining).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática B1–B2
Wikipedia: Spanish subjunctive — subordinate clauses

drills.sentence: Te lo digo para que lo {{answer}}.
drills.translation: I'm telling you so that you know.
Even if it rains, we will go.

drills.sentence: Habla despacio para que todos te {{answer}}.
drills.translation: Speak slowly so that everyone understands you.
Even if it's late, I will finish the work.

drills.sentence: Te dejo la llave para que {{answer}} entrar.
drills.translation: I'm leaving you the key so that you can get in.

drills.sentence: No saldremos hasta que {{answer}} de llover.
drills.translation: We won't go out until it stops raining.

points.title: Perfect subjunctive (haya hablado)
points.explanation: Haya, hayas… + participle appears after present-tense triggers about completed things: Espero que hayas llegado bien (I hope you've arrived safely), No creo que lo haya hecho (I don't think he's done it).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática C1–C2
Wikipedia: Spanish subjunctive — perfect

drills.translation: I hope you have arrived safely.
I don't think he has done it.
I'm glad you have come.
It's possible they have already left.

drills.sentence: No creo que ella haya {{answer}} eso.
drills.translation: I don't think she has said that.

drills.sentence: Dudo que lo {{answer}} terminado a tiempo.
drills.translation: I doubt we have finished it on time.

points.title: Pluperfect subjunctive + conditional perfect
points.explanation: The unreal past pairs si + hubiera + participle with habría + participle: Si hubiera estudiado, habría aprobado (Had I studied, I would have passed). Hubiera can replace habría colloquially in the result clause.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática C1–C2

drills.translation: If I had studied, I would have passed.

drills.sentence: Si me lo hubieras dicho, te {{answer}} ayudado.
drills.translation: If you had told me, I would have helped you.

drills.sentence: Si lo {{answer}} sabido, no habría venido.
drills.translation: If I had known, I wouldn't have come.

drills.sentence: Si hubieras salido antes, no {{answer}} perdido el tren.
drills.translation: If you had left earlier, you wouldn't have missed the train.
I wish you had told me earlier.

drills.sentence: Sin tu ayuda, no lo {{answer}} conseguido.
drills.translation: Without your help, we wouldn't have managed it.

points.title: Passive with ser + participle
points.explanation: Formal/written passive: ser + participle agreeing with the subject, agent with por — La novela fue escrita en 1967; Los puentes fueron construidos por los romanos. Everyday speech prefers the se-passive.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática C1–C2
Wikipedia: Spanish grammar — passive voice

drills.translation: The novel was written in 1967.

drills.sentence: Los puentes fueron {{answer}} por los romanos.
drills.translation: The bridges were built by the Romans.

drills.sentence: El puente fue {{answer}} en el siglo XIX.
drills.translation: The bridge was built in the 19th century.

drills.sentence: Las cartas fueron {{answer}} por la secretaria.
drills.translation: The letters were sent by the secretary.

drills.sentence: La ley {{answer}} aprobada por el parlamento.
drills.translation: The law was passed by parliament.

drills.sentence: El ladrón fue {{answer}} por la policía.
drills.translation: The thief was arrested by the police.

points.title: Cleft sentences (fue… quien / lo que)
points.function: Put the spotlight exactly where you want it
points.explanation: Emphasis via ser + relative: Fue María quien lo dijo (It was María who said it), Es eso lo que me preocupa (That's what worries me), Fue en Madrid donde nos conocimos.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática C1–C2
Wikipedia: Cleft sentence

drills.translation: It was María who said it.
That is what worries me.
It was in Seville that we met.

drills.sentence: Es el dinero lo {{answer}} le importa.
drills.translation: It's money that matters to him.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} mi hermana quien encontró las llaves.
drills.translation: It was my sister who found the keys.

drills.sentence: Fue ayer {{answer}} me enteré de la noticia.
drills.translation: It was yesterday that I heard the news.

points.title: Concessive intensifiers (por más que, digan lo que digan)
points.explanation: High-register concession runs on the subjunctive: Por más que insistas, no lo lograrás (However much you insist…); reduplicated subjunctive: Digan lo que digan, seguiré (Whatever they say, I'll carry on); sea como sea (be that as it may).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática C1–C2

drills.sentence: Por más que {{answer}}, no lo lograrás.
drills.translation: However much you insist, you won't manage it.
Whatever they say, I will carry on.

drills.sentence: Por mucho que {{answer}}, no cambiará nada.
drills.translation: However much you cry, nothing will change.
Whatever happens, I'll be with you.
Whatever the weather, we'll go out.

drills.sentence: Por más que lo {{answer}}, no lo entiendo.
drills.translation: However much I try, I don't understand it.

points.title: Literary -se subjunctive and ojalá + past
points.explanation: The -se forms (hablase, tuviese) are interchangeable with -ra in subordinate clauses and mark a literary register: Si él supiese la verdad… Ojalá + past subjunctive voices unlikely wishes: Ojalá pudiese venir (If only he could come).

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática C1–C2
Wikipedia: Spanish subjunctive — forms

drills.translation: If he knew the truth, he would keep quiet.
If only he could come.
If you asked me, I would help you.
If only I had more time to read.
I wish everything were as before.

drills.sentence: Si él {{answer}} aquí, todo sería distinto.
drills.translation: If he were here, everything would be different.

points.title: No es que + subjunctive (nuanced denial)
points.function: Deny one framing while asserting another
points.explanation: 'No es que + subjunctive, es que + indicative' rejects an interpretation and substitutes the real one: No es que no quiera, es que no puedo (It's not that I don't want to — I can't). The denied clause is subjunctive; the asserted one indicative.

references.title: Plan Curricular del Instituto Cervantes: Gramática C1–C2

drills.sentence: No es que no {{answer}}, es que no puedo.
drills.translation: It's not that I don't want to — it's that I can't.

drills.sentence: No es que me {{answer}} mal, es que no lo conozco.
drills.translation: It's not that I dislike him — I just don't know him.

drills.sentence: No es que no te {{answer}}, es que estoy ocupado.
drills.translation: It's not that I don't believe you, it's that I'm busy.

drills.sentence: No es que {{answer}} tarde, es que hay tráfico.
drills.translation: It's not that it's late, it's that there's traffic.

drills.sentence: No es que no me {{answer}} la fiesta, es que estoy cansado.
drills.translation: It's not that I don't like the party, it's that I'm tired.

drills.sentence: No es que {{answer}} miedo, es que soy prudente.
drills.translation: It's not that I'm afraid, it's that I'm careful.
